message.content:
this is a clean repo. I want to build a custom dashboard for git-ai work, to see my sessions, my data, my stats and so on, everything depending on the data collected by Git AI, like the personal dashboard (https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/personal-insights). The following snipped is extracted from a Perplexity search on the Git AI operation and where the data might be saved: Git AI is an open‑source Git extension that tracks **which lines of your code were generated by AI agents**, preserves that attribution through all Git operations (rebase, squash, merge, etc.), and stores the provenance data in a way that integrates with standard Git workflows.  Below is a comprehensive breakdown of how it works and what data it saves. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)

***

### Overall operation model

Git AI hooks into your normal Git flow by:

1. **Agent‑side hooks**  
   Supported coding agents (e.g., Cursor, Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, or any hook‑compatible agent) call `git ai checkpoint` before and after editing files. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)
   - A **pre‑edit** checkpoint marks everything changed since the last agent edit as **human**‑authored. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)
   - A **post‑edit** checkpoint marks the newly inserted/changed hunks as **AI**‑authored. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)

2. **Git integration**  
   When you run `git commit`, Git AI takes the accumulated checkpoints and writes **AI authorship metadata** into **Git notes** attached to the commit. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)
   These notes survive `rebase`, `squash`, `merge`, `cherry‑pick`, `reset`, and other history‑rewriting operations. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)

3. **No workflow change**  
   You keep using Git as usual; Git AI piggybacks on existing commands and does not clutter your commit messages or diffs. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)

***

### How LINE‑BY‑LINE attribution is built

Git AI never “guesses” what is AI‑generated; instead it relies on **explicit marking by agents**:

- **Incremental checkpoints**  
  - Each agent invocation produces a small **diff** between the previous state and the current working copy. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)
  - Git AI marks each hunk as either **AI‑authored** or **human‑authored**. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)
  - These checkpoints are temporary and stored under `.git/ai/*` until the next commit. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

- **Blame‑aware replay for history edits**  
  When you run `rebase`, `cherry‑pick`, or similar operations, Git AI: [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)
  - Replays the diffs of the original commits.  
  - Uses **blame‑style logic** to map existing AI‑attributed lines into the new commit order.  
  - Produces updated checkpoints that feed into the new **Authorship Log** (Git notes).  

This means Git awareness of AI‑generated lines is preserved even if you rewrite the branch history. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

***

### Data stored and where

Git AI keeps three main kinds of data, each with a different lifecycle and purpose. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

#### 1. Working logs (temporary, repo‑local)

- **Location:**  
  - Directory under `.git/ai/working_logs/<base_commit_sha>/` for each commit generation. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

- **Contents:**  
  - Incremental checkpoints: diffs between the working index and the last agent edit, annotated as AI/human. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)
  - Intermediate state that lets Git AI know which hunks are AI vs human when you finally run `git commit`. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)

- **Lifecycle:**  
  - Deleted after you commit the changes. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

#### 2. Git notes (permanent, repo‑wide)

- **Location:**  
  - Git notes stored under `refs/notes/ai` (a notes ref, not a branch). [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

- **Contents – “Authorship Log”**  
  For each commit, the notes encode: [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)
  - **Which lines** in which files are AI‑generated.  
  - **Which agent** produced them (e.g., Cursor, Claude Code, Copilot, custom agent).  
  - **Which model** or configuration was used (where the agent reports it).  
  - **Prompts or prompt IDs** tied to the AI‑generated hunks (via agent hooks). [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

- **Lifecycle / persistence:**  
  - Notes live in your Git repo: they are **committed metadata**, not in objects like blobs or trees. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)
  - They are pushed and pulled with `git push` / `git fetch` (once notes are pushed), so AI provenance travels with the repo. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)

#### 3. Internal user‑scoped database (optional, local)

- **Location:**  
  - Typically in `~/.git‑ai/internal.db` (SQLite‑based). [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

- **Contents:**  
  - Indexed **prompt records** and associated metadata (e.g., timestamps, agent/model, project). [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)
  - Intent is to let you query “what prompts produced this code?” across multiple repos. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

- **Lifecycle:**  
  - User‑scoped, not pushed to the repo; persists across repos and sessions on that machine. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

#### 4. CAS / upload queue (optional, async)

- **Location:**  
  - SQLite table in `~/.git‑ai/cas_sync_queue`. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

- **Purpose:**  
  - Queue for asynchronously uploading checkpoint data or prompts to a remote CAS (content‑addressable store), if you’re using a hosted analytics/backend layer. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)
  - Transient: cleared once uploads succeed or are retried. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)

***

### How Git AI behaves under common operations

Git AI is designed so that **AI authorship stays correct** even when you rewrite history:

- `rebase`  
  - Git AI replays the diffs of each original commit, using blame‑style logic to preserve AI/human boundaries in the rebased history. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)
  - New commits get new Authorship Logs in `refs/notes/ai`. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

- `squash` / `fixup`  
  - All AI‑attributed lines from the squashed commits are folded into the new combined commit’s authorship data. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

- `merge`  
  - Authorship Logs from each side are carried into the merge commit; you can see which lines came from AI in each branch. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

- `cherry‑pick`  
  - Git AI uses blame to find AI‑generated lines in the picked files and re‑associates them to the new commit’s notes. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

- `reset`  
  - If you reset and then modify or re‑commit, Git AI copies AI attribution from the old notes into checkpoints, so the code that remains in the working copy keeps its AI provenance when you next commit. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

***

### What you can query or inspect

Thanks to this structure, you can:

- Run `git ai status` to see what AI‑authored vs human‑authored hunks are staged/uncommitted. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)
- Query **Authorship Logs** (Git notes) to ask:  
  - “Which lines in this commit are AI‑generated?”  
  - “Which agent/model wrote this block?”  
  - “Which prompts were used for this code?” (if the agent exposes that). [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/docs/cli/how-git-ai-works)

Because the data lives in Git notes and a local SQLite index, you can also build:

- Editor plugins that color AI‑vs‑human lines. [deepwiki](https://deepwiki.com/git-ai-project/git-ai)
- CI checks that require human review for AI‑generated code. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)
- Auditing tools mapping prompts to final production code. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com/blog/introducing-git-ai)

***

### Summary, in your context

If you are a CLI‑heavy, Git‑centric developer, Git AI effectively:

- Adds a **line‑level authorship overlay** on top of Git, stored in lightweight notes and internal logs. [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)
- Makes AI‑generated code **traceable for the full SDLC**, surviving all the usual Git gymnastics you use (rebase, squash, merge). [usegitai](https://usegitai.com)

If you tell me which agents you use (e.g., Claude Code, Copilot, or your own custom agents), I can sketch concrete `.git/hooks` or agent‑hook configs and a minimal schema for how you’d query “AI‑vs‑human” for your Clojure / JS projects. I want the project to be a Bun program that I can run in the CLI and access the UI via a web browser in the localhost.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- prompt-analysis: Analyze AI prompting patterns and acceptance rates
- git-ai-search: Search and restore AI conversation context from git history

QUICK DECISION:
- User mentions ONE ticket/URL…

git notes --ref=refs/notes/ai list 2>/dev/null | head -20 || echo "No ai notes found"; git notes --ref=ai list 2>/dev/null | head -20 || echo "No ai notes (short ref) found"

input.command: sqlite3 ~/.git-ai/internal/db ".schema prompts" 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; sqlite3 ~/.git-ai/internal/db ".schema cas_sync_queue" 2>/dev/null; echo "---"; sqlite3 ~/.git-ai/internal/db ".schema cas_cache" 2>/dev/null
input.description: Get schema of internal db tables

content.content:
CREATE TABLE prompts (
        id TEXT PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL,
        workdir TEXT,
        tool TEXT NOT NULL,
        model TEXT NOT NULL,
        external_thread_id TEXT NOT NULL,
        messages TEXT NOT NULL,
        commit_sha TEXT,
        agent_metadata TEXT,
        human_author TEXT,
        total_additions INTEGER,
        total_deletions INTEGER,
        accepted_lines INTEGER,
        overridden_lines INTEGER,
        created_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
        updated_at INTEGER NOT NULL
    );
CREATE INDEX idx_prompts_tool
        ON prompts(tool);
CREATE INDEX idx_prompts_external_thread_id
        ON prompts(external_thread_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_prompts_workdir
        ON prompts(workdir);
CREATE INDEX idx_prompts_commit_sha
        ON prompts(commit_sha);
CREATE INDEX idx_prompts_updated_at
        ON prompts(updated_at);
---
CREATE TABLE cas_sync_queue (
        id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
        hash TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
        data TEXT NOT NULL,
        metadata TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}',
        status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending' CHECK(status IN ('pending', 'processing')),
        attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
        last_sync_error TEXT,
        last_sync_at INTEGER,
        next_retry_at INTEGER NOT NULL,
        processing_started_at INTEGER,
        created_at INTEGER NOT NULL
    );
CREATE INDEX idx_cas_sync_queue_status_retry
        ON cas_sync_queue(status, next_retry_at);
CREATE INDEX idx_cas_sync_queue_hash
        ON cas_sync_queue(hash);
CREATE INDEX idx_cas_sync_queue_stale_processing
        ON cas_sync_queue(processing_started_at) WHERE status = 'processing';
---
CREATE TABLE cas_cache (
        hash TEXT PRIMARY KEY NOT NULL,
        messages TEXT NOT NULL,
        cached_at INTEGER NOT NULL
    );

        attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,

    );

CREATE TABLE cas_cache (

aiTitle: Build Git AI personal dashboard with Bun

content.text: Now I have a complete picture of the data. Let me call advisor before designing the architecture.

input.description: Check bun version and current project state

content.text:
You're at a good handoff point — orientation done, no commits made yet. A few things to flag before you call advisor or start building:

**Critical constraint not stated in the task:** `~/.git-ai/internal/db-wal` and `metrics-db-wal` exist, meaning the git-ai daemon is actively writing. Open both SQLite files **read-only** (`new Database(path, { readonly: true })`) — never let the dashboard hold a write lock or it can corrupt git-ai's state. Worth saying out loud before any code lands.

**Anchor on the stable schema, not the reverse-engineered one.** The `prompts` table is self-documenting and has everything you need for v1: `tool`, `model`, `workdir`, `commit_sha`, `total_additions/deletions`, `accepted_lines`, `overridden_lines`, `created_at`, `external_thread_id`. The `metrics` table's `event_json` uses opaque integer keys (`"e":1, "v":{"0":0,"1":0,"2":53,...}`) — that's clearly an internal compact encoding, undocumented and version-fragile. Treat metrics-db as a secondary/optional source; don't bake numeric-key decoding into your core views.

**Three data surfaces, three lifetimes — pick what to use:**
1. `prompts` table — sessions, stats, project breakdown ← start here
2. `refs/notes/ai` per-repo — line-level attribution (needs iterating workdirs from prompts) ← phase 2
3. `metrics` events — telemetry stream, brittle keys ← skip unless prompts is missing something

**`prompts.messages` is probably large JSON.** Don't `SELECT *` in list views; defer to a detail route.

**Stack:** Bun 1.3.13 has built-in `Bun.serve` and `bun:sqlite` — for a personal dashboard you don't strictly need Hono, but it's fine. Server-rendered HTML beats a build pipeline here. The dir is empty, so `bun init` then go.

**Suggested phasing for the advisor call:** ask whether to (a) start with prompts-only views and add git notes later, or (b) try to unify prompts + git notes from the start. The trade-off is complexity vs. line-level fidelity in v1.

Now's a good moment to call `advisor()` with the data model you've discovered and your proposed architecture, before writing the first file.
Good. I'll focus on the `prompts` table as the stable foundation, use `bun:sqlite` read-only, and skip the brittle `metrics` numeric keys for v1. Let me build this now.

content.text: The auto-generated CLAUDE.md is fine to keep as-is. Now let me save a project memory and verify the final file structure is clean.

input.description: Check final file structure and line counts

input.content:
---
name: git-ai-dash project
description: Personal dashboard for visualizing Git AI session data, built with Bun + Hono
type: project
---

Dashboard that reads Git AI data from `~/.git-ai/internal/db` (SQLite, readonly) and serves a localhost web UI.

**Why:** User wanted a personal dashboard for their Git AI usage stats — sessions, AI lines written, acceptance rate, per-project breakdown, and conversation history.

**Stack:** Bun 1.3.13 + Hono + `bun:sqlite` (readonly). No build step — server-rendered HTML with Chart.js from CDN.

**Data sources:**
- `~/.git-ai/internal/db` — `prompts` table: sessions, lines, acceptance, model, workdir, messages (full conversation JSON)
- `~/.git-ai/internal/metrics-db` — skipped (opaque numeric keys, version-fragile)
- `refs/notes/ai` — skipped for v1 (line-level attribution, requires iterating repos)

**Routes:**
- `/` — Overview: stats cards, activity line chart, top projects bar chart, recent sessions
- `/sessions` — Paginated sessions list with prompt preview
- `/sessions/:id` — Session detail with conversation thread

**How to apply:** Open `~/.git-ai/internal/db` with `readonly: true` always — the git-ai daemon holds a WAL write lock. Never open write mode.

content.text:
The dashboard is ready. Here's what was built:

**3 files in `src/`:**
- `db.ts` — typed read-only queries against `~/.git-ai/internal/db` (WAL-safe, never writes)
- `views.ts` — server-rendered HTML with dark GitHub-style theme + Chart.js charts
- `index.ts` — Hono routes + Bun server

**Run it:**
```sh
bun start          # or: bun run src/index.ts
# → http://localhost:3333
```

**What you get:**
- **Overview** — 4 stat cards (sessions, AI lines, acceptance rate, projects), a dual-axis activity chart (sessions + lines over time), horizontal bar chart of top projects, and a recent sessions table with first-message previews
- **Sessions** (`/sessions`) — paginated full list with tool badge, model badge, line counts, and acceptance rate
- **Session detail** (`/sessions/:id`) — full metadata + attribution stats + rendered conversation thread

The `accepted_lines` field shows 0% currently because Git AI tracks that data via a separate reconciliation step — it will populate as you accumulate more commits.
